coredata.dc:title: Orange carbon dots based smart sensing platforms for rapid, visual, quantitative identification of sodium copper chlorophyllin
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Xiao
Zheng, Min
coredata.dc:description: A highly affordable, sensitive and portable detection platform for the quantitative identification of sodium copper chlorophyllin (SCC) in food and environment is a crucial need. Even though many carbon dots (CDs) based sensors have been developed, few reports on using CDs as optical probes for SCC detection have been published so far. In this paper, orange luminescent CDs (OLCDs) were prepared via solvothermal method, which have high fluorescence quantum yield (27.20 %) and excellent photostability. OLCDs can detect SCC via inner filter effect (IFE), with fast response, high selectivity, outstanding sensitivity and superior anti-interference ability. Benefiting from the remarkable properties of OLCDs, a portable sensing platform was triumphantly constructed, which facilitated the in situ, real-time quantitative determination of SCC in diverse actual samples, by catching the fluorescence change of OLCDs-based paper sensors via smartphone RGB colorimetric analysis. This first CDs-based smart sensing system displays great potential for quantification of SCC in various fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Optical probe
Sodium copper chlorophyllin
Visual detection
Portable sensing platform

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Response mechanism of the OLCDs probe
Smartphone sensing system for the detection of SCC
Detection of SCC in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characterization of OLCDs. (A) TEM image. (B) Diameter distribution. (C) XRD spectrum. (D) FTIR spectrum. (E) XPS survey scans. (F) Element content. HR XPS of (G) C1s, (H) N1s and (I) O1s.

Optical properties of OLCDs. (A) UV–vis absorption spectrum. Inset: the images of OLCDs solution under visible light (left) and UV light of 365 nm (right). (B) FL excitation and emission spectra. (C) Emission spectra of OLCDs at various excitation wavelengths. The FL intensity variation of OLCDs (D) under LED lamp irradiation and (E) in NaCl. (F) FL spectra of OLCDs in different pH.

(A) FL spectra of OLCDs with different concentration of SCC. (B) Relationship of the FL intensity of OLCDs vs SCC. (C) Selectivity (pink column) and competitive (purple column) detection for SCC. (D) The FL intensity variation of OLCDs in response to SCC over time.
(A) Time-resolved FL decay curves. (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of SCC and FL spectrum of OLCDs. (C) Schematic of the mechanism of OLCDs sensing SCC.

(A) Selectivity and (B) competitive detection of SCC by paper sensors. (C) Schematic diagram of the process to detect SCC by the smartphone sensing system. (D) Paper sensors with varying concentration of SCC. (E) The linear relationship between G/R value and SCC concentration. (F) The G/R value of the paper sensor during the storage for one month.
The preparation of (A) OLCDs and (B) Smart sensing platform based on smartphone-assisted visual sensor and application in the detection of SCC.
The determination of SCC in the actual samples.

head.ce:title: Orange carbon dots based smart sensing platforms for rapid, visual, quantitative identification of sodium copper chlorophyllin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin, 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin, 130022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly affordable, sensitive and portable detection platform for the quantitative identification of sodium copper chlorophyllin (SCC) in food and environment is a crucial need. Even though many carbon dots (CDs) based sensors have been developed, few reports on using CDs as optical probes for SCC detection have been published so far. In this paper, orange luminescent CDs (OLCDs) were prepared via solvothermal method, which have high fluorescence quantum yield (27.20 %) and excellent photostability. OLCDs can detect SCC via inner filter effect (IFE), with fast response, high selectivity, outstanding sensitivity and superior anti-interference ability. Benefiting from the remarkable properties of OLCDs, a portable sensing platform was triumphantly constructed, which facilitated the in situ, real-time quantitative determination of SCC in diverse actual samples, by catching the fluorescence change of OLCDs-based paper sensors via smartphone RGB colorimetric analysis. This first CDs-based smart sensing system displays great potential for quantification of SCC in various fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Sodium copper chlorophyllin (SCC) is a semi-synthetic green pigment prepared from natural chlorophyll after hydrolysis and complexation with copper salt [1,2]. SCC has higher stability, water-solubility, and accessibility than most food colorants, which has been widely used as a food additive globally [3–5]. As a consequence of the potential hazards of copper toxicity, large intakes can cause heavy metal poisoning and cardiovascular diseases [5,6]. For the sake of safety, establishing an effective and accurate method to detect SCC is of utmost importance. So far, the strategy of high-performance liquid chromatography combining with mass spectrometry has been developed for qualitative identification of SCC [7]. However, the great obstacles such as sophisticated instruments and time-consuming operation procedures have prevented their widespread application. Consequently, there is a demand to establish a low-cost, quick, eco-friendly and highly sensitive approach for SCC detection.

Carbon dots (CDs), have attracted a great deal of attention, due to the advantages of unique optical properties [8,9], superior stability [10], and good biocompatibility [11,12]. CDs have extensive applications in the areas of sensing [13–17], bioimaging [18–22], cancer therapy [23–27] and anticounterfeiting [28–32]. Especially, CDs have been successfully proved to be totipotential nanoprobes in identifying and detecting ions [13–15], organic pollutants [17,33] and bioactive substances [34–36], based on the fluorescence (FL) changes of CDs that induced by the mechanisms of aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) [37], photo-induced electron transfer (PET) [38, 39], Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [40,41] and internal filtering effect (IFE) [42,43], etc. However, according to our knowledge, rare research has been done for sensing SCC by CDs.

Herein, orange luminescent CDs (OLCDs) were synthesized via solvothermal method by using perylene-3,4,9,10-tetracarboxylic dianhydride and L-lysine as precursors (Scheme 1 ). The FL lifetime and quantum yield of OLCDs is 4.44 ns and 27.20 %, respectively. The FL of OLCDs can be quenched by SCC instantly, due to IFE. Therefore, we proposed the concept of using OLCDs as FL sensor to specifically detect SCC with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.92 μM. In addition, a mobile device was integrated with OLCDs-based paper sensor to create a portable sensing platform for rapid, real-time, on-location detection of SCC. Furthermore, OLCDs and the smart sensing platform were also successfully applied for quantitative assay of SCC in food and environment. Compared with the previous methods, OLCDs and the smart sensing platform have the advantages of simple preparation, rapid detection and high sensitivity, and can be widely used in the detection of SCC in different actual samples, which have important significance for human health and environmental protection.

Perylene-3,4,9,10-tetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA), L-lysine (L-lys), Sodium copper chlorophyllin (SCC), L-Cysteine (L-Cys), L-glutamic acid (L-Glu), L-arginine (L-Arg), sodium tripolyphosphate (STPP), MnSO4·H2O, Mg(NO3)2, CaCl2, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, KCl, Na2S2O3·5H2O, Na2SO3, NaHSO3, NH4F, NaCl and cytosine (Cyt) were acquired from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) was purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagents Factory (Tianjin, China). Ultrapure water was prepared from a Milli-Q system (Millipore, USA). Honey, hard candy and drink were purchased from local supermarket. Snow water was obtained by melting snowflakes.

OLCDs were synthesized by solvothermal method via using PTCDA and L-lysine as starting materials and DMF as solvent. First, PTCDA (50 mg) was dispersed into DMF (10 mL), then L-lysine (60 mg) was mixed and stirred for 10 min. Afterward, the solution was transferred to stainless-steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and heated to 160 °C for 5 h. After naturally cooled to room temperature, the mixture underwent centrifugation at 7000 rpm for 10 min and dialyzed (MWCO = 500 Da) for 12 h. The OLCDs aqueous solution was vacuum freeze-dried for 24 h to get purplish-red solid powder.

The detection of SCC was specifically examined in an aqueous solution containing OLCDs under ambient conditions at pH = 7.0 at room temperature. SCC (2 μmol) was transferred into a beaker, mixed with 3 mL of deionized water, and moved into a volumetric flask to fix the volume to 10 mL to obtain the SCC (200 μM) standard solution. Standard solution of other substances (Mn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, K+, S2O3 2−, SO3 2−, HSO3 −, F−, Cl−, STPP, Cyt, L-Glu, L-Arg and L-Cys) was prepared in the same way. The OLCDs (20 μg mL−1) solution was mixed with all standard solution in equal volume (1 mL). The FL spectra were measured using excitation wavelength of 531 nm.

The OLCDs-based paper sensor was prepared by using filter paper (1.0 × 3.0 cm) and OLCDs solution. The filter paper was completely submerged in OLCDs solution (10 μg mL−1) for 2 min, then removed and naturally air dried. After that, 500 μL of SCC solution with different concentration (5–25 μM) were added dropwise onto these paper sensors and dried at room temperature. Photographs were taken under UV light in dark environment with smartphone. The color recognition extraction software on the smartphone (downloaded from Xiaomi App Store) was utilized to obtain the RGB values of the images. To ensure the reliability of the data, we conducted three parallel experiments to obtain the average value of G/R.

In real life, the human body primarily absorbs SCC through water and food consumption. Tap water, snow water, hard candy, drink and honey were chosen to evaluate the universality of OLCDs in detecting SCC. The solution underwent centrifugation at 7000 rpm for 10 min, followed by filtration through microporous membranes with a pore size of 0.22 μm, without any other treatment to ensure the authenticity of the actual samples. In order to simulate the residual contamination of SCC, we infused actual samples with various concentration of SCC solution. The actual samples were tested by the methods described above (FL sensing system, smartphone sensing system) and the standard method. To make the experimental results more convincing, three parallel experiments were done for each group to obtain the average value. The SCC in the test solution was assessed by calculating its recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD).

The dimension and shape of OLCDs were characterized by TEM (Fig. 1 A). OLCDs were presented in quasi-spherical shapes with good dispersion, the size was distributed within the region of 1.5–5.5 nm, with an average size of 3.01 ± 0.64 nm (Fig. 1B). The XRD spectrum of OLCDs (Fig. 1C) shows a sharp diffraction peak at 2θ = 26°, indicating that OLCDs have graphitized structures [17]. The FTIR was employed to analyze the surface functional groups of OLCDs (Fig. 1D). The broad peaks at 3430 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of the hydrophilic groups O–H and N–H, verifying the good water solubility of OLCDs [14]. Peaks at 3065 and 2932 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of Ar–H and –CH2–, while peaks at 1659, 1405, 1254 and 1024 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of N–CO, CC, C–N and C–O [17,44]. In addition, the elemental composition of OLCDs was further investigated by XPS spectra. Fig. 1E–F demonstrates that OLCDs are primarily made up of C, N, and O, accounting for 78.63 %, 5.11 %, and 16.26 %, respectively. The curve of high-resolution (HR) C1s is fitted into three peaks at 284.8, 286.1 and 288.6 eV, which originate from CC/C–C, C–O–R and CO (Fig. 1G), respectively [19]. HR N1s shows the presence of C–N and N–H at 399.6 and 400.2 eV (Fig. 1H) [22]. The two peaks of O1s at 531.9 and 533.4 eV are associated with C–O and CO (Fig. 1I) [22]. The results of XPS are basically consistent with FTIR.

Taking into account of the unique structure of OLCDs, their optical properties were further investigated. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of OLCDs (Fig. 2 A) presents a broad absorption peak at 500–540 nm, which are attributed to the n-π* transition of CO [45]. OLCDs have good water solubility in ultrapure water, forming a pale pink transparent solution with an orange FL when exposed to a 365 nm UV lamp (photo inserted in Fig. 2A). The FL spectrum of OLCDs (Fig. 2B) reveals that two maximum emission peaks present at 549 and 586 nm under 531 nm excitation. The luminescence of OLCDs might originate from PTCDA (Fig. S1). With a rise in the excitation wavelength from 490 to 540 nm, despite variations in the FL intensity of OLCDs, there is no observable shift in the emission peak position. This suggests that the emission behavior of OLCDs is none excitation-dependent (Fig. 2C). The FL quantum yield of OLCDs in ultrapure water was 27.20 %, demonstrating that OLCDs have excellent optical properties.

Good FL stability is essential for probes [46]. The FL intensity of OLCDs was found to be almost unchanged after storage for 30 d (Fig. S2), which is very favorable to real-time monitoring over a long period. In addition, the FL intensity of OLCDs was maintained above 98 % even after being exposed to continuous irradiation from a 5W LED lamp for a duration of 6 h (Fig. 2D). Remarkably, in the NaCl solution (600 μM), the FL loss is still below 2 % (Fig. 2E), indicating its ideal capacity to resist ionic interference. The FL intensity of OLCDs was found to be enhanced within the pH range from 2.0 to 7.0, while weakened at pH 7.0–8.0 (Fig. 2F). In all of the subsequent experiments, the pH was maintained at 7.0. The above results indicate that OLCDs have excellent photostability across various experimental settings, which is beneficial for application in complicated environments.

The ability of OLCDs in detecting SCC was investigated. The FL intensity of OLCDs gradually decreased as the SCC concentration increased (Fig. 3 A). When the SCC concentration reached 200 μM, the loss of FL intensity exceeded 98 %. A Stern-Volmer plot was generated by investigating the relationship between FL intensity and SCC concentration within the range of 5–25 μM, I0/I-1 = 0.0372[SCC]-0.1331, R2 = 0.9994, where I0 and I is the FL intensity of OLCDs in the absence and presence of SCC, respectively (Fig. 3B). The calculated LOD for SCC was 0.92 μM (N = 3), indicating that OLCDs have perfect sensing ability for SCC. When different substances were added to the OLCDs solution, the FL of OLCDs was quenched only in the presence of SCC, indicating that OLCDs have excellent selectivity for SCC (Fig. 3C). When other substances were incorporated into the system of OLCDs and SCC, the FL intensity did not change markedly, suggesting that the specific detection of SCC by OLCDs was not interfered with other substances. The detection performance of various methods is shown in Table S1. Compared with the previous methods, OLCDs have the advantages of simple preparation, outstanding specificity and superior anti-interference. Moreover, OLCDs have a rapid response (<1 min), which indicates that OLCDs can be used for real-time detection of SCC (Fig. 3D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of the OLCDs probe
ce:section.ce:para: Given the multitude of surface functional groups present on OLCDs, they can be specifically recognized with target analytes via different mechanisms [46]. Therefore, we conducted a sequence of experiments to investigate the reaction mechanism of OLCDs in the detection of SCC. The time-resolved FL decay curves of OLCDs were measured with or without SCC (Fig. 4 A), and the corresponding FL lifetime of OLCDs was 4.44 and 4.43 ns, respectively. The unsignificant lifetime change implied that it was a static quenching process [47]. In addition, with the FL spectrum of OLCDs exhibits a strong overlap with the UV–vis absorption spectrum of SCC (Fig. 4B). Thus, the FL quenching may originate from IFE (Fig. 4C) [17].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone sensing system for the detection of SCC
ce:section.ce:para: For more visual detection of SCC, paper sensors based on OLCDs were prepared. When different substances (100 μM, 500 μL) were dropped uniformly onto the paper sensors, only SCC could quench the FL of the paper sensors (Fig. 5 A). For the purpose of estimating the specific identification of SCC by the sensor, the solution of other substances was slowly added to the paper sensors that had been quenched by SCC, no obvious FL change was found (Fig. 5B), demonstrating that the OLCDs-based paper sensor has strong anti-interference ability. A portable smartphone sensing platform was developed by using the paper sensor (Fig. 5C), which allowed for real-time, on site, rapid and accurate quantification of SCC. As depicted in Fig. 5D, the increase in SCC concentration resulted in a noticeable decrease in the FL intensity of the sensors. The photos were analyzed with smartphone software to obtain the corresponding RGB values. The SCC concentration exhibited a strong correlation with the value of G/R of the sensor, G/R = 0.0220[SCC]+0.6682, R2 = 0.9952, and the LOD was 1.46 μM (Fig. 5E). It was noteworthy that even after one month of storage, the paper sensor's G/R value did not change significantly (Fig. 5F), indicating that the paper sensor has good stability and can be utilized for long-term tracking SCC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of SCC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to simulate the residual contamination of SCC, the standard addition method was used to add SCC to the real samples for detection [48]. For the OLCDs sensing system and smartphone sensing system, as illustrated in Fig. S3 and Table 1 , when different concentration (5, 10 and 15 μM) of SCC were introduced into the real samples, the recoveries of SCC in the samples exhibited a range 95.21 %–116.74 % and 96.32 %–115.32 %, respectively, which are similar with the results of standard method (Fig. S4 and Table S2), indicating OLCDs sensing system and smartphone sensing system have high accuracy. These findings have significant implications for environmental research and public health.

Orange emissive OLCDs with favorable brightness and high photostability were synthesized, whose FL could be quenched by SCC via IFE. Within a minute, the OLCDs demonstrated their capability as an innovative optical probe for selective detection of SCC and the LOD was 0.92 μM. To make the detection procedure more convenient and faster, the first CDs-based smartphone-assisted sensing system was constructed by uniting the paper sensors with a smartphone RGB color analysis technique, which facilitated real-time, on-location, visual detection of SCC level, without necessitating any supplementary apparatus. Furthermore, both the OLCDs sensing system and smartphone sensing system were successfully utilized to detect SCC in various actual samples such as tap water, snow water, hard candy, honey and drink, with high accuracy and reliability. This work provides an original direction for readily establishing CDs-based optical and smartphone-assisted sensing platforms, as well as expands their applications in food and ecological detection.
Xiao Jin: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Min Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51873023), the Science and Technology Development Plan of Changchun City (No. 21ZY38).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2024.126090.
